clinical_history: This ECG from a middle-aged woman brought to the Emergency Department having been found comatose is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm at about 85/min. Artifact is present, which in some leads mimics a premature ventricular complex . The most notable findings are prolongation of QT/QTc (460/484msec, by Hodges formula) and somewhat prominent positive anterior T waves. Brain imaging studies showed a massive subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). Serum potassium at the time was normal. While the classic ECG associated with intracranial bleeds consists of prominent T wave inversions, with a long QTc and often sinus bradycardia (~CVA T wave pattern~), broad upright T waves with QT prolongation have also been reported. The mechanism of the long QTc in SAH is uncertain; sympathetic nervous system hyperactivity has been suggested. Some patients develop torsade(s) de pointes. An extensive literature exists on proposed cerebro-cardiac connections and ECG findings in various CNS diseases.  Hypercalcemia shortens the QTc. Tricyclic overdose may prolong the QTc and the QRS, but is usually associated with sinus tachycardia. Neither hyperkalemia (tall, narrow T waves with a wide QRS in more advanced stages) nor severe hyperglycemia (changes usually related to serum potassium) account for the observed findings here.
